pg2-p1-s2: The drive for success was on.
pg2-p1-s3: I'd prove to the world I was important.
pg2-p1-s4:
My work took me about Wall Street and little by little I became interested in
the market.

law.

pg2-p1:
I took a night law course, and obtained employment as investigator for a surety company. The drive for success was on. I'd prove to the world I was important. My work took me about Wall Street and little by little I became interested in
the market. Many people lost money - but some became very rich. Why not I? I studied economics and business as well as
law. Potential alcoholic that I was, I nearly failed my law course. At one of the finals I was too drunk to think or
write. Though my drinking was not yet continuous, it disturbed my wife. We had long talks when I would still her
forebodings by telling her that men of genius conceived their best projects when drunk; that the most majestic
constructions of philosophic thought were so derived.
pg2-p2-s1: By the time I had completed the course, I knew the law was not for me.
pg2-p2-s2:
The inviting maelstrom of Wall Street had me in
its grip.
pg2-p2-s3: Business and financial leaders were my heroes.
pg2-p2-s4:
Out of this alloy of drink and speculation, I commenced to
forge the weapon that one day would turn in its flight like a boomerang and all but cut me to ribbons.
pg2-p2-s5:
Living modestly,
my wife and I saved $1,000.
pg2-p2-s6: It went into certain securities, then cheap and rather unpopular.
pg2-p2-s7:
I rightly imagined that
they would some day have a great rise.
pg2-p2-s8:
I failed to persuade my broker friends to send me out looking over factories and
managements, but my wife and I decided to go anyway.
pg2-p2-s9:
I had developed a theory that most people lost money in stocks
through ignorance of markets.
pg2-p2-s10: I discovered many more reasons later on.
pg2-p2:
By the time I had completed the course, I knew the law was not for me. The inviting maelstrom of Wall Street had me in
its grip. Business and financial leaders were my heroes. Out of this alloy of drink and speculation, I commenced to
forge the weapon that one day would turn in its flight like a boomerang and all but cut me to ribbons. Living modestly,
my wife and I saved $1,000. It went into certain securities, then cheap and rather unpopular. I rightly imagined that
they would some day have a great rise. I failed to persuade my broker friends to send me out looking over factories and
managements, but my wife and I decided to go anyway. I had developed a theory that most people lost money in stocks
through ignorance of markets. I discovered many more reasons later on.
pg2-p3-s1:
We gave up our positions and off we roared on a motorcycle, the sidecar stuffed with tent, blankets, a change of
clothes, and three huge volumes of a financial
